title: Agitation, sweating, and twitching after a medication change

chart.oneLiner: 34M brought in by his partner with several hours of agitation, sweating, and jerking movements that began after a recent medication change.

opening: I feel really wired and shaky… my legs keep jerking and I can't stop sweating.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 34-year-old man, restless and diaphoretic, anxious and hypervigilant, mildly agitated but redirectable. Speaks in short bursts, fidgety. Partner is at bedside and can give collateral.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks the partner/chart/medication list/recent changes): He takes an antidepressant for depression (an SSRI, sertraline) and was started two days ago on tramadol by an urgent-care clinician for a back strain. Yesterday he also took several over-the-counter doses of dextromethorphan cough syrup for a cold. Symptoms started this morning, roughly 12 hours after the most recent doses, and have been worsening rapidly. No alcohol or street drugs. No fevers or sick contacts before this. No new antipsychotics or anti-nausea (no metoclopramide/haloperidol).

HPI (from patient — anxious, brief): feels 'wired,' shaky, hot, and sweaty; his legs and feet keep twitching/jerking on their own; heart is racing; some nausea and loose stools today. Denies headache, neck pain, photophobia, focal weakness, or numbness when asked. Denies chest pain.
PMH: depression (on an SSRI), recent back strain. MEDS (if asked): sertraline daily; tramadol started 2 days ago; OTC dextromethorphan since yesterday. SOCIAL: occasional alcohol, no recreational drugs, no recent travel.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: anxious, diaphoretic, agitated, flushed; tremulous. Pupils dilated (mydriasis).
- Vitals: BP 158/96, HR 124, RR 24, 38.9°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Neuro reflexes: brisk, hyperreflexic; sustained clonus at the ankles, greater in the lower extremities than upper; spontaneous myoclonic jerks.
- Motor/tone: increased tone/rigidity, more prominent in the lower extremities; no focal weakness.
- Mental status: agitated, hypervigilant, oriented but restless; no neck stiffness.
- Skin: hot, moist/diaphoretic, flushed; no rash, no dryness.
- Lungs: clear. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Bowel sounds: hyperactive.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Provide the medication/recent-change COLLATERAL only when the student explicitly asks about medications, recent medication changes, new drugs, OTC/cough remedies, or asks the partner/checks the chart.
- Clonus, hyperreflexia, rigidity, diaphoresis, and mydriasis are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer (e.g., no neck stiffness, no focal deficit, skin not dry).

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 104 — normal (excludes hypoglycemia as the cause).
labs.CK: Creatine kinase 1,150 (elevated) — reflects neuromuscular hyperactivity; watch for rhabdomyolysis.
labs.BMP: Na 140, K 4.2, HCO3 19, BUN 18, Cr 1.2 (mild), Glu 104 — mild metabolic acidosis.
labs.Tox: Acetaminophen, salicylate, and ethanol levels negative; urine tox negative for cocaine/amphetamines (helps exclude sympathomimetic toxicity).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm with no acute ischemic ST/T changes and a normal QT interval.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm with no acute ischemic ST/T changes and a normal QT interval. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with normal intervals

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: No consolidation, effusion, or edema (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: No consolidation, effusion, or edema; clear lung fields.
searchTerms: normal chest radiograph PA clear lungs

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this agitation with neuromuscular hyperactivity (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Serotonin syndrome (leading,7). Neuroleptic malignant syndrome — NMS (CANT-MISS,4). Anticholinergic toxicity (CANT-MISS,3). Sympathomimetic toxicity / stimulant intoxication (CANT-MISS,3). Meningitis/encephalitis (CANT-MISS to consider,2). Thyroid storm / malignant hyperthermia (core,2). Names the toxidrome reasoning — recent serotonergic drug change plus clonus/hyperreflexia/hyperthermia (core,4).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Serotonin syndrome. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); Anticholinergic toxicity; Sympathomimetic/stimulant toxicity; Meningitis/encephalitis. Also reasonable: Thyroid storm; Malignant hyperthermia.

coreActions.item: Names the toxidrome reasoning — recent serotonergic drug change plus clonus/hyperreflexia/hyperthermia

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick glucose (CANT-MISS,3). Toxicology / drug levels — acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol, urine tox (CANT-MISS,3). Creatine kinase to assess for rhabdomyolysis (CANT-MISS,3). BMP/renal function and acid-base (2). CBC (1). TSH to exclude thyroid storm (1). Consider CT head / LP if meningitis-encephalitis cannot be excluded clinically (bonus,2). PENALTY: anchoring on the diagnosis without excluding hypoglycemia or co-ingestants (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Fingerstick glucose; Toxicology / drug levels (acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol, urine tox); Creatine kinase.

criticalActions.item: Toxicology / drug levels (acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol, urine tox)

bonusActions.item: CT head / LP if meningoencephalitis cannot be excluded clinically

penalties.item: anchoring on the diagnosis without excluding hypoglycemia or co-ingestants

requiredTests: Toxicology / drug levels (acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol, urine tox)
optionalTests: CT head / LP if meningoencephalitis cannot be excluded clinically
contraindicatedTests: anchoring on the diagnosis without excluding hypoglycemia or co-ingestants
orderSensitiveActions: anchoring on the diagnosis without excluding hypoglycemia or co-ingestants

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia, normal QT. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — sinus tachycardia (7); no acute ischemic changes and a normal/non-prolonged QT (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia or missing the QT (-3).
keyFindings: no acute ischemic changes; comments on a normal QT interval given the serotonergic/co-ingestant context
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemia and a normal QT interval.
commonMistakes: over-calling ischemia from rate-related ST changes
failing to assess the QT interval in a poisoned patient
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ischemic ST/T changes and a normal (non-prolonged) QT interval.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemia and a normal QT interval.
coreActions: no acute ischemic changes; comments on a normal QT interval given the serotonergic/co-ingestant context

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal/clear film (6); notes it screens for aspiration/pneumonia/edema in the agitated hyperthermic patient (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: notes it screens for aspiration/pneumonia or pulmonary edema in the agitated, hyperthermic patient
criticalFindings: No consolidation, effusion, or edema (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).
steps.idealAnswer: No consolidation, effusion, or edema; clear lung fields (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: No consolidation, effusion, or edema (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).

coreActions.item: notes it screens for aspiration/pneumonia or pulmonary edema

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given a recent serotonergic medication change, normal glucose, negative co-ingestant tox, normal TSH, an elevated CK, and an exam with lower-extremity-predominant clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation, diaphoresis, and hyperthermia, what is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Serotonin syndrome (meets Hunter criteria — serotonergic agent plus clonus/hyperreflexia) (6). Explicitly excludes NMS (lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, neuroleptic exposure, slower onset), anticholinergic toxicity (dry skin/absent bowel sounds), sympathomimetic toxicity (negative tox), and CNS infection (no meningismus, normal glucose) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Serotonin syndrome — it meets the Hunter criteria, with a recent serotonergic medication change plus lower-extremity-predominant clonus and hyperreflexia, alongside agitation, diaphoresis, and hyperthermia. Excluded: NMS (no lead-pipe rigidity/bradyreflexia or neuroleptic exposure, and onset is too rapid), anticholinergic toxicity (skin is diaphoretic, not dry), sympathomimetic toxicity (negative co-ingestant tox), and CNS infection (no meningismus, normal glucose).

bonusActions.item: Cites Hunter criteria and contrasts NMS (rigidity/hyporeflexia, slower onset) versus serotonin syndrome (clonus/hyperreflexia, rapid onset)

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH toxicology/serotonin syndrome: immediately STOP all serotonergic agents (CANT-MISS,7); benzodiazepines for agitation, clonus, and autonomic control (CANT-MISS,6); aggressive external cooling and supportive care — IV fluids, monitor for rhabdomyolysis/renal injury (4); cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) for moderate-severe cases not responding to benzodiazepines (3); for severe hyperthermia (>41.1°C) or rigidity, intubate, sedate, and paralyze with a non-depolarizing agent — control temperature (3); Poison Control / toxicology consult and admit, ICU if severe (2). PENALTY: giving antipyretics such as acetaminophen for the centrally mediated hyperthermia (ineffective) (-2); using physical restraints alone, which worsens hyperthermia and lactic acidosis (-2); giving a serotonergic antiemetic (e.g., metoclopramide) or a depolarizing paralytic (succinylcholine) with risk of hyperkalemia in rhabdomyolysis (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Serotonin syndrome: immediately discontinue all serotonergic agents (sertraline, tramadol, dextromethorphan). Benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam/diazepam) for agitation, clonus, and to blunt autonomic instability. Aggressive supportive care: IV crystalloid, external cooling, continuous monitoring, and treat/anticipate rhabdomyolysis and AKI. Cyproheptadine for moderate-to-severe cases not controlled by benzodiazepines. For severe hyperthermia (>41.1°C) or refractory rigidity: rapid sequence intubation with sedation and non-depolarizing paralysis, then active cooling. Avoid antipyretics (centrally mediated fever), avoid physical restraints alone. Consult Poison Control/toxicology; admit, ICU for severe disease.

criticalActions.item: Benzodiazepines for agitation/clonus and autonomic control

coreActions.item: Aggressive external cooling and supportive care (IV fluids, monitor for rhabdomyolysis/AKI)
Intubate, sedate, and paralyze (non-depolarizing) for severe hyperthermia/rigidity

bonusActions.item: Poison Control / toxicology consult and appropriate disposition (ICU if severe)

penalties.item: Physical restraints alone (worsens hyperthermia and acidosis)

requiredActions: Benzodiazepines for agitation/clonus and autonomic control
optionalActions: Aggressive external cooling and supportive care (IV fluids, monitor for rhabdomyolysis/AKI)
Intubate, sedate, and paralyze (non-depolarizing) for severe hyperthermia/rigidity
Poison Control / toxicology consult and appropriate disposition (ICU if severe)
unsafeActions: Physical restraints alone (worsens hyperthermia and acidosis)
steps.disposition: Admit; ICU if severe (hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 34M brought in by his partner with several hours of agitation, sweating, and jerking movements that began after a recent medication change. The diagnosis is Serotonin syndrome, reached by eliciting the focused medication history, performing the targeted neuromuscular exam (clonus, hyperreflexia, rigidity), and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Recent addition of tramadol (a serotonergic agent) to a maintenance SSRI, plus OTC dextromethorphan
Rapid onset within ~12-24 hours of the medication change
Triad of mental status change, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular hyperactivity
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neuro reflexes: brisk, hyperreflexic; sustained clonus at the ankles, greater in the lower extremities than upper; spontaneous myoclonic jerks.
Motor/tone: increased tone/rigidity, more prominent in the lower extremities; no focal weakness.
General: anxious, diaphoretic, agitated, flushed; tremulous. Pupils dilated (mydriasis).
Skin: hot, moist/diaphoretic, flushed; no rash, no dryness.
Mental status: agitated, hypervigilant, oriented but restless; no neck stiffness.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including the medication change and sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal clonus, hyperreflexia, rigidity, and diaphoresis.
Build a differential led by Serotonin syndrome while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes (NMS, anticholinergic, sympathomimetic, CNS infection).
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (glucose, tox, CK) and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Serotonin syndrome and manage with stopping serotonergic agents, benzodiazepines, cooling/supportive care, cyproheptadine, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Physical restraints alone (worsens hyperthermia and acidosis)
managementPearls: Benzodiazepines for agitation/clonus and autonomic control
criticalWorkupItems: Toxicology / drug levels (acetaminophen, salicylate, ethanol, urine tox)
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemia and a normal QT interval.
No consolidation, effusion, or edema (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).
criticalManagementItems: Benzodiazepines for agitation/clonus and autonomic control
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemia and a normal QT interval.
No consolidation, effusion, or edema (obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia and screen the agitated, hyperthermic patient).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Sinus tachycardia with no acute ischemia and a normal QT interval.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives — a normal QT and clear lungs are meaningful in the poisoned, hyperthermic patient.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
